name: Kitsune
description:
Kitsune are intelligent, magical fox spirits in Japanese folklore. They possess paranormal abilities that increase with age and wisdom. A kitsune's most notable ability is shapeshifting, often taking the form of a beautiful woman to trick humans, though they can assume other forms as well. They are believed to possess great intelligence, long lifespans, and potent magical abilities. The number of tails a kitsune has—up to nine—indicates its age, wisdom, and power. Nine-tailed foxes (kyūbi no kitsune) are considered exceptionally powerful and wise. Kitsune are often associated with Inari, the Shinto deity of rice, agriculture, and prosperity, serving as her messengers. They can be benevolent or mischievous, depending on their nature and how they are treated. Stories depict them as tricksters, loyal guardians, or wise advisors. 

Their magic encompasses illusions, mind control, and the ability to breathe fire, among other things. Some kitsune are said to live in trees, and their lairs are believed to be gateways to another world.
subtitle: Magical fox spirits of Japanese folklore
appearance: Typically appear as foxes, often with multiple tails (ranging from one to nine), increasing in number with age and power. Their fur color varies but is commonly red, white, or black. In human form, they are often depicted as beautiful women or handsome men.
habitat: Forests, mountains, and near Shinto shrines dedicated to Inari. They are sometimes said to live within trees or in hidden dens.
behavior: Kitsune are known for their intelligence and cunning. They can be mischievous tricksters, benevolent guardians, or wise advisors. Their behavior often depends on their age, power, and relationship with humans. Some seek to deceive and manipulate, while others are loyal and helpful.

keyMyths.title: The Fox Wife
keyMyths.description: A common tale involves a kitsune transforming into a beautiful woman and marrying a human man. The man often discovers her true nature, leading to various outcomes, such as the kitsune leaving or remaining with him out of love.

keyMyths.title: Kitsune's Fire
keyMyths.description: Kitsune are known for their ability to generate fox-fire (kitsune-bi), mysterious lights that can lead travelers astray or mark their presence. These lights are often seen in forests at night and are attributed to the kitsune's magic.
keyMyths.source: Kwaidan: Stories and Studies of Strange Things by Lafcadio Hearn

keyMyths.title: The Nine-Tailed Fox
keyMyths.description: The kyūbi no kitsune is an incredibly powerful and wise fox spirit with nine tails. It is said to possess vast knowledge and magical abilities, and its presence can bring either great fortune or great misfortune, depending on its nature.

keyMyths.title: Kitsune Messengers of Inari
keyMyths.description: Many kitsune serve as messengers of Inari, the Shinto god of rice, agriculture, and prosperity. These kitsune are often benevolent and protect shrines and their worshippers. They are symbols of good fortune and abundance.

sources.author: Hearn, Lafcadio
sources.work: Kwaidan: Stories and Studies of Strange Things
sources.description: A collection of Japanese folk tales, including several stories about kitsune and their interactions with humans.
sources.text: A collection of Japanese folk tales, including several stories about kitsune and their interactions with humans.

sources.author: Tyler, Royall
sources.description: A comprehensive anthology of Japanese folk tales, providing valuable insights into the cultural significance of kitsune.
sources.text: A comprehensive anthology of Japanese folk tales, providing valuable insights into the cultural significance of kitsune.

sources.author: Reider, Noriko T.

sources.description: Explores the broader context of supernatural creatures in Japanese folklore, offering a comparative understanding of kitsune.

sources.description: Examines the roles of foxes in Japanese religion and culture.
